perturbation.original_text: 1.4 “Confidential Information” means any data or information that is proprietary to the Discloser relating to each party’s respective business or affairs (including, but not limited to, current and future products, designs, business plans, operations, k now-how, technologies, customers, employees, procurement, contracts, research and development, manufacturing processes and plans, marketing strategies, financial information, intellectual property, and related information) disclosed to Recipient after the effective date of this agreement by the Discloser or any third party on behalf of Discloser, whether in written, oral or any other tangible or intangible form. Confidential Information includes any information generated by (or for) Recipient that contains, reflects, or is derived from any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.4 “Confidential Information” means any data or information that the Discloser subjectively deems confidential, relating to each party’s respective business or affairs (including, but not limited to, current and future products, designs, business plans, operations, k now-how, technologies, customers, employees, procurement, contracts, research and development, manufacturing processes and plans, marketing strategies, financial information, intellectual property, and related information) disclosed to Recipient after the effective date of this agreement by the Discloser or any third party on behalf of Discloser, whether in written, oral or any other tangible or intangible form. Confidential Information includes any information generated by (or for) Recipient that contains, reflects, or is derived from any of the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the definition of 'Confidential Information' to rely on the Discloser's 'subjective' belief broadens the definition beyond what's legally protected. This creates an ambiguity because trade secrets laws require objective measures of confidentiality and reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, not just a subjective belief.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification introduces subjectivity, conflicting with the objective standard required for trade secret protection under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA). The UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, not just a subjective belief that information is confidential.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 'subjective' standard for defining 'Confidential Information,' while trade secret law, as explained, requires an objective standard, specifically 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.' The altered contract makes confidentiality dependent on the Discloser's belief, not on objective measures, thus weakening the legal protection typically afforded to trade secrets. The law snippets are unhelpful.

perturbation.original_text: 2.1 Recipient agrees to treat Confidential Information confidential and use the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential and proprietary information of similar importance (but in no event less than reasonable care). Recipient warrants that it will take all necessary cautions to prevent the unauthorized use, disclosure, publication or dissemination of Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.1 Recipient agrees to treat Confidential Information confidential and use the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential and proprietary information of similar importance (but in no event less than reasonable care). Recipient warrants that it will take only some cautions to prevent the unauthorized use, disclosure, publication or dissemination of Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'all necessary cautions' to 'only some cautions,' the agreement weakens the standard of care required to protect Confidential Information. This may contradict the 'reasonable efforts' standard required for trade secret protection, potentially rendering the protection unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The 'reasonable efforts' standard for trade secret protection, as codified in the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, is undermined by allowing the Recipient to take 'only some cautions.' This implies a lower level of protection than legally required to maintain trade secret status.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'all necessary cautions' to 'only some cautions' weakens the standard of care required to protect Confidential Information. The Law Explanation states this undermines the 'reasonable efforts' standard for trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines trade secrets and refers to their protection, suggesting that weakening the protection measures could jeopardize trade secret status, which creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1 Any failure or delay to enforce any provision of this agreement shall not constitute a waiver thereof or of any other provision, unless the respective party expressly waives its right to enforce such provision in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 Any failure or delay to enforce any provision of this agreement shall constitute a waiver thereof or of any other provision, unless the respective party expressly waives its right to enforce such provision in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall not constitute a waiver" to "shall constitute a waiver" significantly alters the enforceability of the contract. It contradicts the general principle of contract law that waiver requires intentional relinquishment of a known right and creates an unintended consequence where inaction can be interpreted as a waiver. This may be unenforceable and contradict established principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Principles of Contract Law - Waiver
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts outlines the requirements for a valid waiver, including intent and knowledge. This modification implies that any failure or delay automatically constitutes a waiver, contradicting the requirement of intentional relinquishment of a known right as stated by the law, undermining contract enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: waiver | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you waiver A waiver is the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege . In legal contexts, a waiver is often documented in writing, but it can also be implied through actions . By waiving a right, an individual or party chooses not to enforce a particular right, claim , or provision they are legally entitled to. [Last reviewed in July of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex accidents and injuries COMMERCE commercial activities LIFE EVENTS accidents & injuries (tort law) THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal procedure business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure individual rights legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the legal definition of 'waiver.' The original text states that inaction does not constitute a waiver, aligning with the principle that waiver requires intent. The changed text reverses this, stating that inaction *does* constitute a waiver. This contradicts the provided snippet from Wex, which defines a waiver as 'the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege.' By stating that failure or delay *constitutes* a waiver, the modified contract eliminates the requirement for intentional relinquishment, creating a legal contradiction as it deviates from the standard legal definition.

perturbation.original_text: 7.1 This agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with Austrian law, excluding its conflict of laws provisions and each party hereby irrevocably s ubmits to the jurisdiction of the courts in Graz, Austria.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.1 This agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with Austrian law, including its conflict of laws provisions and each party hereby irrevocably s ubmits to the jurisdiction of the courts in Graz, Austria.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "excluding its conflict of laws provisions" to "including its conflict of laws provisions" introduces uncertainty because conflict of laws rules could lead to the application of another jurisdiction's laws if Austrian conflict of laws rules so dictate. This directly contradicts the intention to have the agreement governed solely by Austrian substantive law, creating legal ambiguity regarding which country's laws apply.
perturbation.law_explanation: By including the conflict of laws provisions, the agreement now subjects itself to the possibility of applying laws other than Austrian law. The Austrian Private International Law Act (IPR-Gesetz) dictates which law governs when there are conflicts, thus potentially overriding the intent for Austrian substantive law to exclusively govern the agreement. The URLS are to legal databases of Austria, but only one is in English.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'excluding its conflict of laws provisions' to 'including its conflict of laws provisions' directly introduces the possibility that laws other than Austrian law may govern the agreement. The Law Explanation states that the Austrian Private International Law Act (IPR-Gesetz) could override the intention for Austrian substantive law to exclusively govern the agreement. The inclusion of conflict of laws provisions opens the door for a different jurisdiction's laws to apply, creating a legal ambiguity. The scraped snippet from JUSLINE Österreich lists the IPR-Gesetz and its sections, confirming its existence and relevance to governing conflict of laws. This supports the claim that including conflict of laws provisions contradicts the intent to have Austrian law exclusively govern, as it introduces external legal influence.
